model_narrative: We place two equally sized armies of queens, one black and one white, on a square chessboard. For every square we decide whether it holds a black queen and whether it holds a white queen, and we track the common size shared by the two armies. The two armies must coexist peacefully, meaning no black queen and white queen may ever be positioned so that they attack each other. The objective is to make the common army size as large as possible.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the positions along one side of the square chessboard, indexed by integers in increasing order; the board is square so this same set indexes both the rows and the columns, and a board square is identified by a (row, column) pair drawn from this set

sets.name: M
sets.doc: the set of conflicting square pairs, given as 4-tuples (row, column, row2, column2); a tuple is present exactly when a black queen placed on the first square and a white queen placed on the second square would attack each other, that is they share the same row, the same column, or lie on a common diagonal. The first two indices refer to the black square and the last two to the white square

vars.name: b
vars.doc: black queen placement indicator; equals 1 if a black queen occupies the square at that row and column and 0 otherwise

vars.name: w
vars.doc: white queen placement indicator; equals 1 if a white queen occupies the square at that row and column and 0 otherwise

vars.name: tot
vars.doc: the common army size, that is the number of queens in each army; both armies are required to hold the same number of queens

constraints.name: b[i,
constraints.intent: For every pair of squares that would let a black queen and a white queen attack each other, the two cannot both be occupied at once. At most one of those two squares may hold its queen, so the black queen and the white queen of any attacking pair are never placed together and the two armies stay at peace.

constraints.name: tot
constraints.intent: Set the common army size equal to the number of black queens placed. Count every black queen on the board and require the army size to match that total.

constraints.name: tot
constraints.intent: Set the common army size equal to the number of white queens placed. Count every white queen on the board and require the army size to match that total, which together with the matching count for black forces both armies to hold the same number of queens.

query: We're looking at whether forcing the black army to keep a foothold on the leftmost file changes how big the armies can grow. Suppose we require black to station at least one of its queens somewhere down the first column, and let the rest of the placement adjust around that — what total army size does the optimizer come back with?
expected_pyomo: model.b_col1_presence = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[i, 1] for i in model.i) >= 1)

query: There's a proposal on the table to let the white army dominate the main diagonal — the run of squares from the top-left corner straight down to the bottom-right. We'd require white to hold strictly more of those diagonal squares than black does. With white forced to lead on the diagonal that way, what's the largest each army can still be?
expected_pyomo: model.w_diag_lead = Constraint(expr=sum(model.w[i, i] for i in model.i) >= sum(model.b[i, i] for i in model.i) + 1)

query: We'd like to see the armies cross territory rather than sit on their own halves: require the black army to put at least one queen somewhere in the bottom row and the white army at least one in the top row. What army size does the model come back with under that mixing rule?
expected_pyomo:
model.b_bottomrow = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[max(model.i), j] for j in model.i) >= 1)
model.w_toprow = Constraint(expr=sum(model.w[min(model.i), j] for j in model.i) >= 1)

query: Imagine the black army is confined to a forward staging area only — the first two rows of the board — while white can still roam anywhere. With every black queen restricted to rows 1 and 2, how large can the matched armies be?
expected_pyomo:
def _b_front_only(m, i, j): return m.b[i, j] <= 0 if i > 2 else Constraint.Skip
model.b_front_only = Constraint(model.i, model.i, rule=_b_front_only)

query: The black army is sprawling all over the upper board. Couldn't it just sit tidily in the top-left quadrant — rows 1 to 4 and columns 1 to 4 — and still field nine queens? If we shut the black army out of everything past that quadrant, what does the model say is actually achievable?
expected_pyomo:
def _b_quadrant(m, i, j): return m.b[i, j] <= 0 if not (i <= (len(m.i) + 1) // 2 and j <= (len(m.i) + 1) // 2) else Constraint.Skip
model.b_quadrant = Constraint(model.i, model.i, rule=_b_quadrant)

query: Why does the white army need to be smeared across the whole lower board? It seems like it could pack neatly into the bottom-right corner block — rows 6 to 8 and columns 6 to 8 — without giving anything up. Shut white out of everything but that corner and tell me what the count really is.
expected_pyomo:
def _w_corner(m, i, j): return m.w[i, j] <= 0 if not (i > len(m.i) - (len(m.i) + 2) // 3 and j > len(m.i) - (len(m.i) + 2) // 3) else Constraint.Skip
model.w_corner = Constraint(model.i, model.i, rule=_w_corner)

query: I keep looking at the black queens fanned out across the board and thinking they'd be just as effective huddled into the top-left 3x3 block. Shouldn't a nine-queen army fit in there? Force every black queen inside rows 1-3 and columns 1-3 and show me where the count actually lands.
expected_pyomo:
def _b_3x3(m, i, j): return m.b[i, j] <= 0 if not (i <= (len(m.i) + 2) // 3 and j <= (len(m.i) + 2) // 3) else Constraint.Skip
model.b_3x3 = Constraint(model.i, model.i, rule=_b_3x3)

query: Column 1 is sitting almost empty on the black side. Couldn't the black army commit a real presence there — say at least four of its queens down the first file — without shrinking the army? What's the optimizer's read on forcing that?
expected_pyomo: model.b_col1_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[i, 1] for i in model.i) >= 4)
